interactions.summary: Kaiser asks for actionable reform prescriptions; Jiang first answers through Camus and Sisyphus, warning that reforming Chinese education may be impossible because education is linked to politics, culture, and society.

Jiang gives a three-part prescription: openness to criticism and new ideas, diversity across schools and local conditions, and a risk-taking culture that empowers principals and educators to experiment.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang answer; Kaiser pushes that the prescriptions feel too large.

speaker_notes.note: Boundary remains awkward around “first of all, the, the school system”; the three intended elements are openness, diversity, and risk-taking.

claims.claim: Jiang compares Chinese education reform to Sisyphus rolling the boulder because education is tied to politics, culture, and society.
The first requirement for reform is openness: Chinese schools must become more open to criticism and new ideas.
The second requirement is diversity because a monolithic pedagogy and textbook system is inappropriate for a country as varied as China.
The third requirement is risk-taking: principals and a new generation of educators must be empowered to try ideas, most of which will fail, until local formulas emerge.
Jiang rejects simply importing AP or International Baccalaureate as the answer; China must discover a formula right for its own local schools.

signature_moments.moment: Chinese education reform is Sisyphus pushing the boulder because the school is tied to everything else.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the prescription a tragic scale: reform cannot be reduced to a classroom technique.

signature_moments.moment: Most new ideas should be allowed to be stupid and fail because failure is how China finds its own school formula.
signature_moments.source_phrase: these new ideas will be stupid and they’ll fail
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang’s pro-experiment reform ethic in its sharpest form.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang’s metaphor for education reform as an apparently futile task linked to the whole social order.
A school culture that lets principals and educators try, fail, and discover locally appropriate models.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR/boundary damage obscures part of the diversity prescription, but the list structure remains clear from surrounding text.
